philosophicalDetails.definition: Divine powers, decrees, fundamental attributes of civilization

connections: Abzu - fresh water abyss beneath earth
name: Me (Divine Powers)
description: Also known as ✨ Me - Divine Powers.

richMetadata.structure: The Anunnaki are organized hierarchically under Anu (sky god). They inhabit multiple levels: high Anunnaki (council in heaven), middle Anunnaki (executive functions), and lower Anunnaki (workers). They gather in Nippur and convene in divine assembly (Ekur) for cosmic decisions.
connections: Abzu - fresh water abyss beneath earth
richMetadata.significance: The Anunnaki represent divine bureaucracy and cosmic administration. They created humans to serve them and maintain cosmic order. Their existence illustrates Sumerian understanding of divine hierarchy, duty, and the interplay between celestial and terrestrial realms. The Anunnaki embody the principle of divine work and governance.
sources: Descent of Inanna - journey through realms
Cuneiform texts from Nippur and Sumeria

keyMyths.title: Inanna's Descent to the Underworld
keyMyths.description: Inanna, Queen of Heaven, descends to the underworld, ruled by her sister Ereshkigal, to expand her dominion. She must relinquish her me one by one at each gate, symbolizing the relinquishment of power, ultimately facing death and resurrection through the intervention of Enki.
keyMyths.source: Inanna's Descent to the Underworld (ETCSL t.1.4.1)

keyMyths.title: Inanna and Enki: The Transfer of the Arts of Civilization from Eridu to Uruk
keyMyths.description: Inanna cunningly obtains the me from Enki, the god of wisdom in Eridu, by intoxicating him. She then transports them to her city of Uruk, establishing its cultural and political supremacy and bringing civilization to its people.

keyMyths.title: The Creation Myth
keyMyths.description: The creation myth describes the separation of heaven and earth, and the creation of humankind. The me are essential to the proper functioning of the cosmos and the roles of deities and mortals within it, establishing order from chaos.

keyMyths.title: The Myth of Adapa
keyMyths.description: Adapa, a wise human created by Enki, is summoned to heaven and offered immortality. He refuses the food of life, influenced by Enki's deceptive advice, thereby condemning humanity to mortality, highlighting the limitations imposed by the me.

keyMyths.title: The Instructions of Shuruppak
keyMyths.description: This wisdom text, attributed to Shuruppak, provides moral and practical advice for living a just and prosperous life. It implicitly acknowledges the existence of the me by outlining the proper societal order and behavior, reflecting the established divine norms.

sources.name: Electronic Text Corpus of Sumerian Literature (ETCSL)
sources.description: A comprehensive online resource providing translations and transliterations of Sumerian literary texts.
sources.title: Electronic Text Corpus of Sumerian Literature (ETCSL)

sources.name: Black, J., Cunningham, G., Flückiger-Hawker, E., Robson, E., & Zólyomi, G. (2006). *The Literature of Ancient Sumer*. Oxford University Press.
sources.description: A standard academic compilation of Sumerian literature with detailed commentary.
sources.title: Black, J., Cunningham, G., Flückiger-Hawker, E., Robson, E., & Zólyomi, G. (2006). *The Literature of Ancient Sumer*. Oxford University Press.

sources.name: Bottéro, J. (2001). *Religion in Ancient Mesopotamia*. University of Chicago Press.
sources.description: An analysis of Mesopotamian religious beliefs and practices.
sources.title: Bottéro, J. (2001). *Religion in Ancient Mesopotamia*. University of Chicago Press.

sources.name: Enuma Elish
sources.description: The Babylonian creation epic, providing insight into Mesopotamian cosmology.
sources.title: Enuma Elish

sources.name: Hallo, W. W., & Younger, K. L. (Eds.). (2000). *The Context of Scripture: Canonical Compositions, Monumental Inscriptions, and Archival Documents from the Biblical World*. Brill.
sources.description: A collection of translated ancient Near Eastern texts, providing context for understanding the Bible and Mesopotamian culture.
sources.title: Hallo, W. W., & Younger, K. L. (Eds.). (2000). *The Context of Scripture: Canonical Compositions, Monumental Inscriptions, and Archival Documents from the Biblical World*. Brill.

worshipPractices.name: Invocation and Recitation
worshipPractices.description: Priests and individuals would invoke the names of deities associated with specific Mes and recite their importance. This practice aimed to harness the power of the Mes for the benefit of the community or individual, ensuring order and prosperity.

worshipPractices.name: Temple Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Offerings were made to temples dedicated to deities who embodied specific Mes. These offerings, including food, drink, and precious objects, were intended to appease the gods and secure their continued support in upholding the order governed by the Mes.

festivals.name: New Year (Akitu) Festival
festivals.description: The Akitu festival celebrated the renewal of the agricultural cycle and the reaffirmation of divine authority. The king would symbolically renew his rule, ensuring the proper functioning of the Mes and the stability of the cosmos.

festivals.name: Inanna's Descent and Return
festivals.description: Re-enactments of Inanna's journey to the underworld and her subsequent return were performed, demonstrating the importance of the Mes related to life, death, and resurrection. These performances served to reinforce the cyclical nature of existence and the power of the divine.

cultural.modernLegacy: The concept of 'Me' as fundamental principles or codes that govern existence resonates with modern systems thinking and data structures. The idea of inherent properties defining objects and their interactions can be seen as a precursor to object-oriented programming and philosophical discussions about inherent qualities.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Ma'at embodies cosmic order, truth, and justice, similar to the 'Me' in that it represents the fundamental principles governing the universe and society.
Dharma represents the cosmic law and order that upholds the universe, akin to the 'Me' as a set of divine decrees that maintain stability and proper functioning.
Wyrd, often personified by the Norns, represents fate and destiny, influencing the course of events and the structure of reality, paralleling the 'Me' as forces that shape existence.
Themis embodies divine law, order, and justice, representing the established rules and principles that govern the cosmos and society, mirroring the 'Me' as fundamental powers.

associations.name: Order
associations.significance: The Me are fundamental to maintaining cosmic and social order.

associations.name: Authority
associations.significance: Possession of the Me grants authority and legitimacy to gods and rulers.

associations.name: Foundation
associations.significance: The Me are the foundational principles upon which civilization is built.

associations.name: Stars
associations.significance: Like stars, the Me are fixed points providing guidance and structure to the world.

associations.name: Seven
associations.significance: Seven is considered a holy number, and the Me are vital aspects of divinity.

associations.name: Destiny
associations.significance: The Me influence the destiny of individuals, cities, and the cosmos.

associations.name: Grain
associations.significance: Grain represents sustenance and the Me govern the prosperity and abundance of the land.

companions.name: Enki
companions.description: Enki, the god of wisdom, often aids in interpreting and utilizing the Me. He understands their complexities and can guide their application for the benefit of civilization.

companions.name: Inanna
companions.description: Inanna, goddess of love and war, famously sought to acquire the Me from Enki, representing a desire to wield divine power and influence.

companions.name: Nisaba
companions.description: Nisaba, the goddess of writing and accounting, is crucial for recording and organizing the Me, ensuring their proper management and preservation.

companions.name: Scribes
companions.description: Skilled scribes are essential to record and document the Me, ensuring their proper storage and dissemination to those deemed worthy.

extendedContent.title: The Nature of Me: Immutable Divine Decrees
extendedContent.content:
In Sumerian mythology, the 'me' (pronounced 'may') are not deities themselves, but rather fundamental, immutable decrees or divine powers that govern all aspects of civilization and the cosmos. They represent the very essence of what makes things what they are, dictating the proper order and function of everything from kingship and craftsmanship to religious rituals and even emotions. Think of them as the operating system of the universe, pre-programmed by the gods. Without the 'me,' chaos would reign, and society would collapse. They are often conceived as tangible objects, precious and jealously guarded, rather than abstract concepts. The exact number and nature of the 'me' varied, but they are generally understood to be essential components of divine authority and societal stability.

These divine powers are not static; they can be transferred or even stolen, as demonstrated in the myth of Inanna and Enki. This highlights the importance of possessing and controlling the 'me' for those seeking power and influence. The 'me' are crucial for understanding Sumerian cosmology and the relationship between the gods and humanity. Their presence ensures order and predictability in a world constantly threatened by chaos. The list of 'me' found in various texts gives a glimpse into the values and concerns of Sumerian society, revealing what they considered essential for a thriving civilization.

The concept of the 'me' provides a framework for understanding how the Sumerians perceived the organization of their world. It demonstrates a belief in a divinely ordained order that permeates all aspects of existence. Studying the 'me' allows us to delve deeper into the Sumerian worldview and appreciate the complexity of their beliefs about the cosmos and the role of humanity within it. The power of the 'me' underlines the importance of maintaining order and adhering to the divine decrees to ensure the stability and prosperity of society.

extendedContent.title: Inanna and Enki: The Theft of the Me
extendedContent.content:
The most famous story involving the 'me' is undoubtedly the myth of Inanna (Ishtar in Akkadian) and Enki, the god of wisdom and water. According to the epic, Enki possessed all the 'me' in his temple city of Eridu. Inanna, desiring to bring civilization and power to her city of Uruk, cleverly tricked Enki into getting drunk and gifting her all the 'me.' This was achieved through a series of drinking games and seductive charm, highlighting Inanna's cunning and ambition.

As Enki sobered up, he realized his grave mistake and sent his demons after Inanna to retrieve the 'me.' However, Inanna, aided by her faithful servant Ninshubur, successfully evaded Enki's attempts and brought the 'me' to Uruk. This event is celebrated as a triumph for Uruk and a demonstration of Inanna's power and resourcefulness. The poem details the specific 'me' that Inanna stole, offering a valuable list of the divine powers considered essential for a prosperous city.

This myth serves not only as an entertaining narrative but also as a symbolic representation of the transfer of power and knowledge from one city to another. It illustrates the importance of the 'me' in maintaining order and prosperity and highlights the ambition of city-states to acquire these divine powers. The story also reveals the complex relationship between the gods, characterized by both cooperation and competition. The consequences of Enki's mistake and Inanna's actions shaped the balance of power in the Sumerian pantheon and contributed to the rise of Uruk as a major center of civilization.

extendedContent.title: Examples of Me and Their Significance
extendedContent.content:
The specific 'me' mentioned in Sumerian texts provide insight into what aspects of life the Sumerians valued and considered essential for a functioning society. These 'me' encompass a wide range of concepts, from concrete skills and practices to abstract principles and emotions. Examples include 'kingship,' 'truth,' 'justice,' 'craftsmanship,' 'writing,' 'priestess-hood,' 'wisdom,' 'victory,' 'destruction,' 'joy,' 'sorrow,' and 'fear.' Each of these 'me' played a crucial role in shaping Sumerian society and its relationship with the divine.

The presence of 'kingship' as a 'me' underscores the importance of a divinely sanctioned ruler in maintaining order and enforcing justice. 'Truth' and 'justice' highlight the moral foundations of Sumerian society, emphasizing the importance of fairness and honesty in interactions between individuals and the state. The inclusion of 'craftsmanship' and 'writing' demonstrates the value placed on skilled labor and the ability to record knowledge and history. The presence of emotions like 'joy,' 'sorrow,' and 'fear' suggests that even these fundamental human experiences were considered to be governed by divine decrees.

By examining the specific 'me' mentioned in Sumerian texts, we can gain a deeper understanding of the values, beliefs, and priorities of this ancient civilization. These divine powers represent the building blocks of Sumerian society, providing a framework for understanding their worldview and their relationship with the gods. The 'me' reveal a complex and nuanced understanding of the world, where both tangible skills and abstract concepts are seen as essential components of a thriving civilization.

extendedContent.title: The Me and the Divine Hierarchy
extendedContent.content:
The 'me' are intrinsically linked to the divine hierarchy of the Sumerian pantheon. While the 'me' themselves are not deities, they originate from the gods and are often associated with specific deities who are seen as their custodians or possessors. For example, Enki, the god of wisdom and water, is often depicted as the guardian of many 'me,' reflecting his role as the source of knowledge and civilization. An, the sky god, may have been seen as the ultimate source of the 'me,' representing the overarching order of the cosmos.

The distribution of the 'me' among the gods reflects the division of responsibilities and powers within the pantheon. Each god is associated with specific domains and functions, and the 'me' that they possess reflect these roles. This system reinforces the idea that the gods are responsible for maintaining order and ensuring the proper functioning of the world. The transfer of 'me' from one god to another, as seen in the myth of Inanna and Enki, highlights the dynamic nature of power and influence within the pantheon.

The concept of the 'me' also helps to explain the power and authority of the gods. By possessing these divine powers, the gods are able to shape the world and influence the lives of humans. The 'me' are the tools that the gods use to enforce their will and maintain order in the cosmos. Understanding the relationship between the 'me' and the divine hierarchy is essential for comprehending the complex workings of Sumerian mythology and the role of the gods in shaping the world.

extendedContent.title: Legacy and Influence of the Me Concept
extendedContent.content:
While the Sumerian civilization eventually faded, the concept of the 'me' left a lasting legacy on Mesopotamian thought and beyond. The idea of divinely ordained principles governing the universe and society influenced later Mesopotamian cultures, including the Akkadians, Babylonians, and Assyrians. These cultures adopted and adapted many aspects of Sumerian mythology, including the concept of the 'me,' which they incorporated into their own religious and philosophical systems.

The influence of the 'me' can also be seen in later philosophical and religious traditions. The idea of fundamental principles governing the universe resonates with concepts such as natural law and divine law found in other cultures. The emphasis on order and stability, embodied in the 'me,' is a recurring theme in many philosophical systems that seek to understand the nature of reality and the proper way to live.

Furthermore, the story of Inanna and Enki continues to fascinate and inspire scholars and artists today. The themes of ambition, cunning, and the transfer of knowledge remain relevant and engaging, offering insights into the human condition and the dynamics of power. The 'me' serve as a reminder of the enduring power of myth to illuminate the values, beliefs, and concerns of past civilizations and to offer guidance and inspiration for the present.
